message:
fix(conformal): HoltPredictor::new snaps NaN α/β to 0.5 instead of preserving via clamp

HoltPredictor::new used `alpha.clamp(0.001, 0.999)` and
`beta.clamp(0.001, 0.999)` to bound the smoothing parameters. Like
the rest of the documented `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` family
(ft-761tz disk_pressure, ft-yskcu continuous_backpressure,
ft-icreu ActivityProfile::new, ft-26k3h ActivityProfile::update_hour,
ft-eqtcq aegis_entropy_anomaly), the clamp does NOT sanitize NaN —
it returns NaN unchanged. A NaN α or β then survives into the
predictor's recurrence:

  self.level = self.alpha.mul_add(value, (1.0 - self.alpha) *
               (self.level + self.trend))

`NaN.mul_add(value, ...)` is NaN regardless of value. The post-hoc
guard at lines 145-148 catches the NaN level/trend and resets to
`value`/`0.0`, so the NEXT update sees the reset state — but the
next update ALSO computes NaN via the same NaN α, then resets again.
The predictor never makes progress beyond the most recent observation;
it silently degrades to "always echo the latest value, no smoothing
or trend modeling at all" without surfacing the misconfiguration.

Fix: at construction time, snap NaN α or β to the midpoint default
(0.5) before clamping. NaN can no longer survive into the smoothing
recurrence. 0.5 is a neutral choice — exactly between the
[0.001, 0.999] bounds and a defensible default for a caller who
clearly fed garbage.

Three regression tests pin: NaN α with finite β; NaN β with finite α;
both NaN. All assert `level()` and `trend()` stay finite after three
real observations.

Verification: code is correct by inspection — mirrors the established
ft-icreu fix pattern. The committed test binary hits the documented
macOS dyld hang under load (per memory: "Newly compiled test binaries
hang at 32 bytes RSS in _dyld_start under system load"); the no-default-
features build itself produces the binary cleanly with 224 warnings,
0 errors. Existing `holt_predictor_clamps_extreme_params` test
unchanged and continues to assert the finite-result contract for
extreme (non-NaN) inputs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
